subtopic_detail: STEMI - reperfusion choice with rural transfer window
stem: A 64-year-old man presents to a small rural emergency department in regional NSW with 70 minutes of central crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw. He has hypertension and is a current smoker. He is in pain but well perfused. The 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) shows 3 mm ST elevation in II, III and aVF with reciprocal depression in I and aVL. Right-sided leads show 1 mm ST elevation in V4R. A high-sensitivity troponin is pending. There are no fibrinolysis contraindications. The closest primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) centre is a 90-minute road transfer; the local helicopter retrieval team estimates first medical contact to balloon at 150 minutes. The nurse asks how to proceed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 58, BP 96/62, SpO2 96% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST elevation 3 mm II/III/aVF, reciprocal depression I/aVL, V4R ST elevation 1 mm.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Aspirin, clopidogrel, enoxaparin, tenecteplase now, then transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours
options.rationale: Inferior STEMI with right ventricular involvement and first medical contact to balloon of 150 minutes exceeds the 120-minute primary PCI window. Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway is weight-banded tenecteplase plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin, followed by transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours later regardless of clinical course.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Transfer directly for primary PCI without fibrinolysis
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred only when first medical contact to balloon is achievable within 120 minutes. At 150 minutes, delaying reperfusion costs myocardium. This would be correct if the PCI centre were within 60 minutes by road (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Aspirin, ticagrelor, enoxaparin, tenecteplase now, then transfer for angiography
options.rationale: Ticagrelor is not used with fibrinolysis because of excess bleeding without ischaemic benefit. Clopidogrel is the preferred P2Y12 inhibitor with lysis. Ticagrelor is appropriate when primary PCI is the reperfusion strategy (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Aspirin and intravenous glyceryl trinitrate, defer reperfusion until troponin returns
options.rationale: Diagnosis is established on the ECG; troponin is not required to act in STEMI. Nitrates can precipitate profound hypotension in right ventricular infarction, where preload-dependence is critical. This option would be inappropriate even outside the RV setting (eTG).

options.text: Aspirin, ticagrelor and unfractionated heparin infusion, observe for spontaneous reperfusion
options.rationale: Conservative management is never appropriate for ST elevation infarction with available reperfusion. Spontaneous reperfusion occurs in a minority and cannot be relied upon. This pathway would be correct only when both lysis and PCI are contraindicated, which is not the case here (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: When primary PCI cannot deliver first medical contact to balloon under 120 minutes, fibrinolysis (tenecteplase) plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin is the pharmaco-invasive standard, with transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours later.
key_points: Reperfusion choice is decided by FMC-to-balloon time, not geography per se
Clopidogrel pairs with lysis; ticagrelor/prasugrel pair with primary PCI
RV infarction is preload-dependent: avoid nitrates and diuretics
Pharmaco-invasive angiography 3-24 h after lysis is mandatory, not optional
explanation.context: Inferior STEMI is most often a right coronary artery occlusion. RV involvement raises mortality and requires fluid loading rather than vasodilatation. The 120-minute FMC-to-balloon cut-off reflects evidence that delays beyond that erode the survival advantage of PCI over lysis.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with two episodes of central chest pain over 6 hours, the last 90 minutes ago at rest. She has type 2 diabetes, hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3a. The 12-lead ECG shows 1.5 mm horizontal ST depression in V4 to V6 with dynamic T-wave inversion. High-sensitivity troponin rises from 62 to 180 ng/L over 3 hours (reference <14). She is comfortable, normotensive and well perfused. The Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events (GRACE) score is 162.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 148/82, SpO2 97% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1.5 mm V4-V6, dynamic T-wave inversion.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate timing of coronary angiography?

options.rationale: The under-2-hour pathway is reserved for refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic or electrical instability, or mechanical complications. None are present here (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.rationale: High-risk NSTEMI (GRACE over 140, dynamic ECG change, troponin rise meeting myocardial infarction criteria) warrants invasive angiography within 24 hours per Heart Foundation/CSANZ. She meets all three criteria.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.rationale: The 72-hour window is for intermediate-risk patients (GRACE 109-140) without dynamic changes. Her GRACE of 162 with dynamic ST changes places her in the early-invasive group (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Selective invasive strategy after stress imaging
options.rationale: Selective/ischaemia-guided strategy is reserved for low-risk presentations (GRACE under 109, no dynamic changes, negative troponin). High-risk biomarker and ECG features override frailty or CKD downgrading (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Defer angiography because of CKD stage 3a
options.rationale: Stage 3a CKD is not a contraindication; high-risk NSTEMI patients with CKD have greater absolute benefit from early invasive care with contrast minimisation. KHA-CARI supports angiography with periprocedural hydration (KHA-CARI; Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: NSTEMI is risk-stratified into four invasive timing tiers: immediate under 2 hours for instability; early under 24 hours for high-risk; delayed under 72 hours for intermediate; selective for low-risk. GRACE over 140, dynamic ECG and troponin rise all push to the 24-hour pathway.
key_points: GRACE >140 plus dynamic ECG = early invasive (<24 h)
Immediate angiography only for instability or refractory ischaemia
Dynamic troponin rise meeting MI criteria is a high-risk feature
CKD is not a barrier; mitigate contrast nephropathy risk
explanation.context: NSTEMI lacks the time-is-muscle imperative of STEMI but still benefits from prompt revascularisation in high-risk subsets. The GRACE score weights age, vitals, ECG and biomarkers to predict in-hospital mortality and is the dominant trigger for the 24-hour pathway in Australian practice.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: Narrow complex tachycardia - stable SVT management
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to a metropolitan emergency department with 45 minutes of palpitations and mild light-headedness that began abruptly while sitting at work. She has no past cardiac history. The 12-lead ECG shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 188 bpm without visible P waves. She is alert and well perfused with cool peripheries; BP 112/74. Continuous monitoring is in place and intravenous access has been obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 188 regular, BP 112/74, SpO2 99% room air, RR 18.
data_table.ECG: Regular narrow complex tachycardia, no visible P waves, QRS 92 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Synchronised direct current cardioversion at 100 J
options.rationale: Electrical cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamic instability (hypotension, ischaemia, altered consciousness, pulmonary oedema). She is stable, so vagal manoeuvres and adenosine come first (eTG).

Stable narrow complex regular tachycardia: vagal manoeuvres are first-line. The modified (REVERT trial) Valsalva with supine repositioning and leg raise terminates approximately 43% of episodes versus 17% for standard Valsalva. Adenosine follows if unsuccessful.

Adenosine is correct after failed vagal manoeuvres in stable SVT. Skipping vagal manoeuvres is the anti-pattern: they are free, painless and frequently effective. Adenosine causes a brief but intensely unpleasant flushing/chest-pain sensation (eTG).

Beta-blockade can terminate SVT but acts slowly and risks hypotension. It is appropriate as a longer-term rate control agent or for atrial tachycardias, not as the first acute step for stable AV-nodal-dependent SVT (eTG).

Amiodarone has no role in stable narrow complex SVT first-line and carries hypotension, bradycardia and long elimination half-life concerns. It is reserved for refractory broad complex or atrial arrhythmias (eTG).

explanation.summary: Stable narrow complex regular tachycardia is managed in a stepwise way: vagal manoeuvres (modified Valsalva first), then adenosine 6 then 12 mg, then a rate-controlling agent. Electrical cardioversion is reserved for instability.
key_points: Modified Valsalva (REVERT): supine + 15-second strain + 15-second leg raise
Adenosine 6 mg then 12 mg if needed via large antecubital cannula with flush
Synchronised DC cardioversion if unstable (start 50-100 J biphasic)
Document a 12-lead during and after termination for diagnostic clues
explanation.context: Most regular narrow complex tachycardias are AV-nodal re-entrant or AV re-entrant tachycardia. Both depend on the AV node for re-entry and respond to vagal tone enhancement. A rhythm strip during termination often reveals the mechanism.

sources.label: REVERT trial - Appelboam et al., Lancet 2015

subtopic_detail: Broad complex tachycardia - stable VT management
stem: A 67-year-old man with prior anterior myocardial infarction and an ejection fraction of 32% presents with 20 minutes of palpitations and chest tightness. The 12-lead ECG shows a regular broad complex tachycardia at 168 bpm with QRS duration 168 ms, northwest axis, concordance across the precordial leads, and capture beats visible in lead II. He is alert, BP 108/68, SpO2 97% room air, no pulmonary oedema. He has tolerated symptoms without syncope or chest pain at rest.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 regular, BP 108/68, SpO2 97% room air, RR 20.
data_table.ECG: Broad complex regular tachycardia QRS 168 ms, northwest axis, precordial concordance, capture beats.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological agent?

options.rationale: Adenosine in broad complex tachycardia of ventricular origin is ineffective and may accelerate the rhythm if a pre-excited atrial fibrillation is the true diagnosis. With prior infarct and clear VT criteria, adenosine is the wrong drug (eTG).

options.text: Intravenous amiodarone 300 mg over 20 to 60 minutes
options.rationale: Stable monomorphic ventricular tachycardia in structural heart disease is treated with intravenous amiodarone 300 mg loading over 20 to 60 minutes, then 900 mg over 24 hours. Lignocaine is an alternative, particularly in ischaemic VT, but amiodarone is first-line in Australian practice.

Verapamil in VT can precipitate haemodynamic collapse through negative inotropy and AV block. It is contraindicated in broad complex tachycardia unless fascicular VT is confirmed by an electrophysiologist (eTG).

Beta-blockade in acute monomorphic VT risks hypotension in a patient with impaired ejection fraction and is not a recognised termination agent. It has a role in suppression after termination or for electrical storm (eTG).
Magnesium is first-line for polymorphic VT/torsades de pointes, not monomorphic VT. The northwest axis with concordance and capture beats describes monomorphic VT (eTG).

explanation.summary: Stable monomorphic VT in a patient with prior infarction is managed with intravenous amiodarone; instability mandates synchronised DC cardioversion. Polymorphic VT/torsades is managed with magnesium and correction of the QT trigger.
key_points: Brugada/Vereckei criteria: concordance, AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats = VT
Stable monomorphic VT: amiodarone 300 mg IV over 20-60 min, then 900 mg/24 h
Unstable VT: synchronised DC cardioversion 100-200 J biphasic
Polymorphic VT/torsades: magnesium 2 g, stop QT-prolonging drugs, overdrive pace
explanation.context: When a broad complex tachycardia presents in someone with structural heart disease, the pre-test probability of VT exceeds 95%. Treating as SVT with aberrancy is the major anti-pattern and can be lethal. Default to VT until proven otherwise.

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with 30 minutes of palpitations and chest pain. The monitor shows a regular broad complex tachycardia at 200 bpm. She becomes increasingly unwell: BP 78/42, cool peripheries, drowsy, with crackles to the mid-zones. Pads are placed and the defibrillator is set to synchronised mode.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 200, BP 78/42, SpO2 92% on 6 L oxygen, RR 28, GCS 14.
data_table.ECG: Regular broad complex tachycardia QRS 160 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial cardioversion energy?

options.rationale: 50 J is reasonable for narrow complex regular tachycardia (atrial flutter) but suboptimal first energy for broad complex VT in an unstable adult (ANZCOR 11.9).

ANZCOR recommends 100 J biphasic synchronised as the initial energy for adult broad complex tachycardia with a pulse, escalating to 150 J then 200 J if unsuccessful. Stacked shocks every 1 to 2 minutes with brief CPR if pulseless arrest develops.
Unsynchronised shock in a perfusing rhythm risks induction of ventricular fibrillation if the shock falls on the T wave. Synchronised mode is mandatory while a pulse is present (ANZCOR 11.9).
Monophasic defibrillators are largely obsolete in Australian practice. If only monophasic available, 200 J synchronised is the starting energy (ANZCOR 11.9).

Pharmacological loading delays definitive treatment in an unstable patient. Electricity is the answer when adverse features are present; antiarrhythmic loading follows successful cardioversion to prevent recurrence (ANZCOR 11.9).

explanation.summary: ANZCOR adverse features (shock, syncope, ischaemia, heart failure) in any tachyarrhythmia mandate synchronised DC cardioversion. Initial biphasic energies: 70-120 J for narrow regular, 120-150 J for narrow irregular, 100 J for broad regular, escalating to 150 then 200 J.
key_points: Adverse features = synchronised DC cardioversion first, not drugs
Synchronised mode prevents R-on-T induction of VF
Sedate the conscious patient briefly (midazolam ± ketamine/propofol)
Pad placement: anterolateral standard, anteroposterior alternative
explanation.context: The synchronised mode delays delivery to coincide with the R wave. Without sync, a shock landing in the vulnerable T-wave period can precipitate VF. Most modern defibrillators revert to unsynchronised mode after each shock and must be re-armed.

subtopic_detail: Polymorphic VT and torsades de pointes
stem: A 56-year-old woman with depression admitted overnight on citalopram and quetiapine develops recurrent self-terminating runs of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with a twisting QRS axis. Between runs the ECG shows sinus rhythm at 52 bpm with corrected QT interval (QTc) 612 ms. Potassium is 3.1 mmol/L and magnesium 0.62 mmol/L. She is conscious between episodes.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 52 sinus between runs, BP 102/64, SpO2 98%, RR 18.
data_table.ECG: Polymorphic VT runs; sinus QTc 612 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Polymorphic VT with a long QT (torsades de pointes) is treated first with intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g irrespective of serum magnesium. Magnesium stabilises the membrane and abolishes early after-depolarisations.
Amiodarone prolongs QT and is contraindicated in torsades. It is appropriate for monomorphic VT (eTG).
Sotalol is a class III antiarrhythmic that further prolongs QT and would worsen torsades (eTG).
Adenosine has no role in polymorphic VT and may transiently prolong QT in susceptible patients (eTG).

Sustained polymorphic VT often cannot be synchronised because of the changing QRS morphology; if persistent or unstable, unsynchronised shock is used (defibrillation). Magnesium is the first pharmacological step in self-terminating runs (ANZCOR 11.9).

explanation.summary: Torsades de pointes management: magnesium 2 g IV, correct potassium to high-normal (4.5-5.0 mmol/L), withdraw QT-prolonging drugs, and consider overdrive pacing or isoprenaline to shorten QT by accelerating heart rate. Defibrillate (unsynchronised) if pulseless.
key_points: Magnesium first, regardless of baseline level
Aim K 4.5-5.0 mmol/L, Mg over 1.0 mmol/L
Withdraw culprit drugs: many psychiatric, antiemetic, antiarrhythmic, macrolide agents
Overdrive pacing at 90-110 bpm shortens QT if drug measures fail
explanation.context: Torsades arises from early after-depolarisations on a prolonged action potential. Bradycardia exposes the long QT and is often the trigger. The classic cocktail in psychiatric inpatients (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor plus atypical antipsychotic plus hypokalaemia) is high-risk.

stem: A 78-year-old man with chronic heart failure and reduced ejection fraction presents with two hours of progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea and pink frothy sputum after running out of his frusemide a week earlier. He is sitting upright, distressed and unable to speak in full sentences. Crackles extend to the mid-zones bilaterally. BP 168/96, HR 118 sinus, SpO2 86% on room air, RR 32. He is alert.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 168/96, SpO2 86% room air, RR 32, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Upright, accessory muscle use, bibasal to mid-zone crackles, raised JVP, peripheral oedema.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial combination?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg, sublingual glyceryl trinitrate, non-invasive ventilation
options.rationale: Acute pulmonary oedema with adequate blood pressure is managed with loop diuretic, vasodilator (nitrate) for preload/afterload reduction, and non-invasive ventilation (continuous positive airway pressure or bilevel) which reduces preload, afterload and work of breathing and lowers intubation rate.

options.text: Intravenous morphine 5 mg, intravenous frusemide 40 mg, intubation
options.rationale: Routine morphine is no longer recommended in acute pulmonary oedema because of associations with increased mortality and intubation. Intubation is reserved for non-invasive ventilation failure or impending arrest (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Intravenous noradrenaline infusion and intravenous frusemide 40 mg
options.rationale: Vasopressors are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension. With BP 168/96 she has hypertensive flash pulmonary oedema requiring vasodilatation (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Intravenous fluid bolus 500 mL and intravenous frusemide 40 mg
options.rationale: Fluid loading worsens pulmonary oedema and is contraindicated. This option pairs an active harm with a correct adjunct (eTG).

options.text: Oral metoprolol 25 mg and intravenous frusemide 40 mg
options.rationale: Beta-blockade is held or reduced in acute decompensated heart failure with congestion. Initiation in the acute setting risks worsening cardiac output (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: The acute pulmonary oedema triad is loop diuretic, nitrate vasodilator, and non-invasive ventilation. Hold beta-blockers, avoid morphine, withhold fluids, treat the precipitant.
key_points: CPAP/BiPAP reduces preload, afterload and work of breathing
Nitrate is the most effective afterload reducer in hypertensive flash APO
Loop diuretic dose: at least the patient's usual daily oral dose given IV
Identify precipitants: ACS, arrhythmia, non-adherence, infection, anaemia
explanation.context: Decompensation in chronic heart failure is precipitated by an identifiable trigger in over 90% of admissions. Non-adherence to diuretics, dietary salt load, new arrhythmia or silent ACS dominate the differential. The first 90 minutes set the trajectory.

stem: A 66-year-old man presents 6 hours after onset of anterior STEMI to a tertiary centre. Primary percutaneous coronary intervention to the proximal left anterior descending artery has restored flow. After return to coronary care he becomes hypotensive with cool, mottled peripheries and oliguria. BP 78/54 despite a 500 mL crystalloid bolus, HR 112 sinus, lactate 4.8 mmol/L, urine output 8 mL/h. Bedside echocardiography shows a severely impaired left ventricle (ejection fraction 20%), no tamponade, no acute mitral regurgitation, no ventricular septal defect. Pulmonary artery catheter shows cardiac index 1.6 L/min/m2, pulmonary capillary wedge 24 mmHg, systemic vascular resistance 1800 dyn s cm-5.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112, BP 78/54, SpO2 94% on 6 L oxygen, RR 26, urine 8 mL/h.
data_table.Haemodynamics: CI 1.6 L/min/m2, PCWP 24 mmHg, SVR 1800 dyn s cm-5.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line vasoactive agent?

options.text: Noradrenaline infusion titrated to MAP 65 mmHg
options.rationale: SOAP II and OptimaCC trials show noradrenaline as the preferred first-line vasopressor in cardiogenic shock compared with dopamine and adrenaline, with lower arrhythmia and refractory shock rates. NHFA endorses noradrenaline to restore perfusion pressure while inotropic support is considered.
Dopamine increases arrhythmia and 28-day mortality in the cardiogenic shock subgroup of SOAP II. It is no longer first-line in Australian practice (NHFA).
OptimaCC was stopped early because adrenaline caused more refractory shock and lactic acidosis than noradrenaline. It is reserved for refractory cases or post-arrest physiology (OptimaCC).
Dobutamine is an inodilator and can drop systemic vascular resistance further, worsening hypotension. It is added to a vasopressor (noradrenaline) once mean arterial pressure is restored (NHFA).
Vasopressin is an adjunct when noradrenaline requirements rise or for catecholamine sparing. It is not first-line in cardiogenic shock (NHFA).

explanation.summary: Cardiogenic shock without a mechanical complication is managed with noradrenaline to restore mean arterial pressure, dobutamine or milrinone for inotropy, treatment of the cause (urgent revascularisation), and consideration of mechanical circulatory support (intra-aortic balloon pump or extracorporeal membrane oxygenation).
key_points: Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor (SOAP II, OptimaCC)
Add inotrope (dobutamine/milrinone) once MAP restored
Exclude mechanical complications: VSD, papillary rupture, free wall, tamponade
Early mechanical support discussion with cardiothoracic ICU
explanation.context: Cardiogenic shock complicates approximately 5-10% of STEMI presentations and remains the dominant cause of in-hospital death after acute infarction. Early revascularisation (SHOCK trial) plus protocolised vasoactive support and mechanical support pathways have improved outcomes.

stem: A 62-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy (ejection fraction 28%) presents with decompensated heart failure and is admitted to the Hunter New England LHD coronary care unit. After 48 hours of intravenous frusemide his weight has fallen 4 kg, BP 118/72, HR 76 sinus, creatinine has improved from 142 to 108 µmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L. He has been euvolaemic for 24 hours. His current medications are perindopril 5 mg and frusemide intravenously. He is asymptomatic at rest.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76, BP 118/72, SpO2 97% room air, afebrile.
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 108 µmol/L, K 4.4 mmol/L, Hb 132 g/L.

lead_in: Which additional therapy is most appropriate to commence before discharge?

options.rationale: NHFA/CSANZ recommends all four pillars of HFrEF therapy (ACE inhibitor or ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitor) initiated during the index admission once euvolaemic and haemodynamically stable. Each provides additive mortality benefit. STRONG-HF demonstrated rapid uptitration during admission halved 180-day rehospitalisation.
Digoxin and ivabradine are second-line for symptomatic relief or rate control after the four pillars are optimised. Neither carries the survival benefit of the foundational therapies (NHFA).
Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are neutral at best in HFrEF; non-dihydropyridines are harmful. Hydralazine-nitrate is reserved for ACE/ARB-intolerant or African ancestry adjunct (NHFA).

options.text: Bisoprolol alone, defer other agents to outpatient
options.rationale: Delayed initiation of MRA and SGLT2 inhibitor was the historic pattern and is now superseded by simultaneous quadruple therapy. STRONG-HF showed in-hospital uptitration is safer and more effective (STRONG-HF).

Substitution offers no incremental benefit. If switching, sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) replaces ACE inhibitor with mortality benefit (PARADIGM-HF), but adding the other pillars is the higher-yield step (NHFA).

explanation.summary: HFrEF foundational therapy comprises four pillars: renin-angiotensin blockade (preferably ARNI), beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor. All four should be initiated and uptitrated before discharge after stabilisation.
key_points: Quadruple therapy initiated simultaneously when stable, not sequenced over months
STRONG-HF: in-hospital rapid uptitration halves 180-day rehospitalisation
SGLT2 inhibitor benefit independent of diabetes status (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR)
Monitor K, creatinine and BP after each titration
explanation.context: The transformation in HFrEF outcomes over the past decade reflects the cumulative effect of four therapies each cutting mortality by approximately 15-25%. Initiating all four at low doses before discharge captures the benefit faster than sequenced outpatient titration.

stem: A 60-year-old man with inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction has 2 mm ST elevation in V4R confirming right ventricular involvement. He is bradycardic at HR 48 and hypotensive at BP 84/52 with clear lungs. The cardiology registrar has given 250 mcg of atropine without effect. There is no pulmonary oedema. Primary PCI has been activated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 48 sinus brady, BP 84/52, SpO2 97% room air, RR 20.
data_table.ECG: II/III/aVF ST elevation 3 mm, V4R ST elevation 2 mm.
data_table.Examination: Clear lungs, raised JVP, no peripheral oedema.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before cardiac catheterisation transfer?

options.text: Intravenous normal saline 250 to 500 mL bolus
options.rationale: Right ventricular infarction produces preload-dependent low output. Intravenous fluid loading restores right ventricular filling pressures and improves left ventricular preload through ventricular interdependence, typically increasing blood pressure rapidly.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.rationale: Nitrates drop preload and can precipitate profound hypotension in right ventricular infarction. They are contraindicated when V4R confirms RV involvement (eTG).
Diuresis worsens the preload-dependent low output state of RV infarction. The lungs are clear; there is no pulmonary congestion to relieve (eTG).
Vasopressor support is reserved for fluid-refractory shock in RV infarction. Fluid loading is the appropriate first manoeuvre (eTG).
AV block in inferior STEMI is usually transient and responds to reperfusion. Pacing is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia unresponsive to atropine and fluid (eTG).

explanation.summary: Right ventricular infarction physiology: a stiff, ischaemic right ventricle cannot generate forward flow without high filling pressures. Treatment is fluid loading, avoidance of preload-reducing drugs, and reperfusion.
key_points: V4R ST elevation = right ventricular infarct in inferior STEMI
Avoid nitrates, diuretics and morphine
Fluid bolus 250-500 mL crystalloid, repeat to clinical response
AV block usually resolves with reperfusion
explanation.context: Up to 40% of inferior STEMIs have RV involvement. Recognising the clear lungs with hypotension pattern prevents the reflex error of giving nitrates or diuretics for what looks superficially like heart failure but is the opposite physiology.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 71-year-old woman with chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) on oral frusemide 80 mg twice daily at home presents with worsening dyspnoea and 5 kg weight gain over 10 days. She is admitted to a general medical ward, comfortable on 2 L oxygen, BP 124/76, HR 88, with crackles to the mid-zones. Renal function is at her baseline (creatinine 116 µmol/L).

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 124/76, SpO2 95% on 2 L oxygen, RR 22.
data_table.Home meds: Frusemide 80 mg PO BD, bisoprolol, perindopril, spironolactone.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial diuretic regimen?

options.rationale: A bolus below her total daily oral dose risks under-diuresis. DOSE-AHF showed equivalent efficacy of 1x versus 2.5x usual oral dose IV, but doses below the usual oral dose are inadequate (DOSE-AHF).

DOSE-AHF supports intravenous loop diuretic at a dose at least equal to the patient's total daily oral dose, typically divided twice or three times daily, or as continuous infusion. Her oral dose is 80 mg twice daily so 80 mg IV twice daily is a reasonable starting dose with response titration.

options.text: Continue oral frusemide 80 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Gut oedema reduces oral bioavailability in decompensated congestion; intravenous administration is preferred during acute decompensation (NHFA).
Thiazide diuretics are weak monotherapy in heart failure congestion. Sequential nephron blockade with a thiazide is added to a loop in diuretic resistance, not initiated alone (NHFA).

options.text: Hold spironolactone and start oral metolazone 5 mg daily
options.rationale: Stopping the mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist removes a survival therapy. Metolazone is a sequential nephron blocker added to a loop in resistance, not first-line (NHFA).

explanation.summary: In decompensated HFrEF, intravenous loop diuretic at a dose at least equal to the total daily oral dose, divided two to three times daily or as infusion. Monitor weight, urine output, electrolytes and renal function daily; escalate or add sequential blockade if response inadequate.
key_points: IV dose at least equal to total daily oral dose (DOSE-AHF)
Switch to IV during congestion: oral absorption is unreliable
Monitor urine output, daily weight, K, creatinine
Sequential blockade (thiazide/metolazone) added for diuretic resistance
explanation.context: Gut wall oedema and splanchnic congestion reduce oral loop diuretic absorption in acute decompensation. The IV route bypasses this. Inadequate initial diuresis predicts prolonged admission and rehospitalisation.

subtopic_detail: Post-MI mechanical complication - acute mitral regurgitation
stem: A 70-year-old woman is on day 4 of admission for inferior STEMI managed with thrombolysis and uncomplicated angiography (single-vessel right coronary artery disease, no intervention required). She suddenly develops severe dyspnoea and pink frothy sputum. BP 92/60, HR 124, SpO2 84% on 8 L oxygen, RR 32. Auscultation reveals widespread crackles and a new soft apical systolic murmur. Bedside echocardiography shows flail posteromedial papillary muscle leaflet with severe mitral regurgitation; left ventricular ejection fraction is preserved at 55%.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, BP 92/60, SpO2 84% on 8 L oxygen, RR 32.
data_table.Echo: Flail posteromedial papillary muscle, severe MR, LVEF 55%.
data_table.Examination: Widespread crackles, soft apical systolic murmur.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent cardiothoracic referral for mitral valve surgery
options.rationale: Acute papillary muscle rupture causing severe mitral regurgitation is a surgical emergency with mortality exceeding 50% medically; emergency mitral valve repair or replacement is the only definitive treatment. Inferior STEMI predisposes via single-supply RCA territory to the posteromedial papillary muscle.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Repeat coronary angiography and percutaneous intervention
options.rationale: Papillary muscle rupture is a mechanical, not ischaemic, complication; the artery has already been revascularised. Cath lab is not the answer (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.rationale: Medical therapy alone in acute papillary muscle rupture is associated with greater than 50% mortality at 24 hours. Diuretics palliate but do not address the structural defect (ESC/EACTS).
Beta-blockade in cardiogenic shock with new severe regurgitation worsens output. The mechanical complication requires surgery, not pharmacological optimisation (ESC/EACTS).

options.text: Intra-aortic balloon pump as definitive treatment
options.rationale: Mechanical support is a bridge to surgery in this setting, not definitive therapy. It reduces afterload while transfer and theatre are arranged but does not repair the valve (ESC/EACTS).

explanation.summary: Mechanical complications of MI (papillary muscle rupture, ventricular septal defect, free wall rupture) typically present 2 to 7 days post-infarct with sudden haemodynamic deterioration. Each is a surgical emergency; medical therapy bridges to theatre.
key_points: Day 2-7 post-MI sudden deterioration = think mechanical complication
Posteromedial papillary muscle has single-vessel (PDA) supply - vulnerable in inferior MI
Echo distinguishes papillary rupture, VSD, free wall rupture, tamponade
Surgery is definitive; medical/mechanical support bridges
explanation.context: The posteromedial papillary muscle receives blood from a single source (the posterior descending artery), whereas the anterolateral papillary muscle has dual supply. This explains why posteromedial rupture is more common after inferior MI. Time-critical surgical referral, not further medical optimisation, defines outcome.

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response - rate vs rhythm
stem: A 64-year-old man with hypertension presents with palpitations of 6 hours duration. He has had a similar episode 12 months ago that self-terminated. ECG shows atrial fibrillation at ventricular rate 142. He is comfortable, BP 142/86, no chest pain, no heart failure features. He has had no oral anticoagulation. CHA2DS2-VASc is 1.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 142 irregular, BP 142/86, SpO2 98% room air, RR 18.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral metoprolol 25 mg as initial rate control
options.rationale: Stable atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and onset under 48 hours: rate control with a beta-blocker is first-line per NHFA/CSANZ atrial fibrillation guidelines. Rhythm control with cardioversion is an alternative but does not improve mortality and requires anticoagulation pre and post.
Electrical cardioversion is reasonable in onset under 48 hours but is not the default first step in a stable patient. Rate control is simpler and avoids procedural risks (NHFA/CSANZ).
Amiodarone is reserved for rate or rhythm control when beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers are contraindicated or ineffective, given its long-term toxicity profile (eTG).
Digoxin is second-line for rate control in patients with heart failure or beta-blocker intolerance, and has slow onset and weak effect during sympathetic activation (eTG).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily for stroke prevention
options.rationale: Aspirin has no role in stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation (NHFA, ESC). At CHA2DS2-VASc 1 in men, oral anticoagulation is considered (not mandated); aspirin is not a substitute.

explanation.summary: Stable atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response is managed first with rate control (beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker). Rhythm control may be added when symptoms persist or in selected groups (younger, first episode, heart failure). Stroke prevention is decided by CHA2DS2-VASc and uses oral anticoagulation, not aspirin.
key_points: Beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine CCB first-line rate control
Digoxin reserved for HFrEF or where rate cannot be achieved
Cardioversion within 48 h onset without TOE if anticoagulation is started
Aspirin is not stroke prevention in AF
explanation.context: Atrial fibrillation affects approximately 2% of Australian adults rising to over 10% above age 75. The decision tree separates rate vs rhythm control, anticoagulation, and reversible precipitant search (sepsis, hyperthyroidism, ischaemia, alcohol).

subtopic_detail: ACS antiplatelet selection with high bleeding risk
stem: A 79-year-old woman has been diagnosed with NSTEMI and proceeds to invasive angiography with drug-eluting stent insertion to the right coronary artery. Her past history includes prior gastrointestinal bleed on dual antiplatelet therapy 3 years ago, current oral anticoagulation with apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 5), eGFR 48, and weight 58 kg. She has no mechanical valve.

data_table.History: Prior GIB on DAPT, AF on apixaban, CHA2DS2-VASc 5, eGFR 48, weight 58 kg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antithrombotic regimen at discharge?

options.text: Aspirin, clopidogrel and apixaban for 12 months, then aspirin plus apixaban
options.rationale: Triple therapy (DAPT plus anticoagulant) beyond a brief peri-procedural window markedly increases bleeding without ischaemic benefit. AUGUSTUS, PIONEER-AF and ENTRUST-AF showed dual therapy (single antiplatelet plus oral anticoagulant) is preferred (NHFA).

options.text: Clopidogrel plus apixaban 5 mg twice daily for 12 months, then apixaban alone
options.rationale: AUGUSTUS demonstrated that omitting aspirin (after a short peri-procedural week or so) and continuing clopidogrel plus apixaban reduces bleeding without increasing ischaemic events. After 12 months, single oral anticoagulant alone is continued. Apixaban dosing 5 mg BD is correct (she meets no two of age >=80, weight <=60, creatinine >=133 µmol/L).

options.text: Aspirin, ticagrelor and warfarin for 12 months
options.rationale: Ticagrelor in combination with anticoagulation is not recommended due to excess bleeding (NHFA). Warfarin offers no advantage over direct oral anticoagulants in this setting (AUGUSTUS).

Stent thrombosis risk after percutaneous coronary intervention is unacceptably high without any antiplatelet for the first 6 to 12 months. A single antiplatelet (clopidogrel) is required with the anticoagulant during this window (NHFA).

options.text: Aspirin and apixaban for 1 month, then apixaban alone
options.rationale: After stent insertion, the antiplatelet of choice in dual therapy is clopidogrel, not aspirin. Continuing aspirin instead of clopidogrel is the historic regimen now superseded (AUGUSTUS).

explanation.summary: Triple therapy after percutaneous coronary intervention in anticoagulated patients is now limited to a brief peri-procedural window; dual therapy (clopidogrel plus oral anticoagulant) for up to 12 months, then anticoagulant monotherapy, is the Australian-endorsed pathway (AUGUSTUS).
key_points: Drop aspirin after the index admission week; continue clopidogrel + apixaban
12 months total of dual therapy, then anticoagulant alone
Apixaban 2.5 mg BD only if two of: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 µmol/L
Avoid ticagrelor + anticoagulant combinations
explanation.context: The triple therapy era assumed additive ischaemic benefit but caused unacceptable bleeding. Four large randomised controlled trials (PIONEER-AF, RE-DUAL PCI, AUGUSTUS, ENTRUST-AF) converged on the dual-therapy approach now adopted in NHFA/CSANZ guidance.

stem: A 54-year-old man with extensive anterior STEMI 8 hours after primary PCI remains in cardiogenic shock despite noradrenaline 0.5 mcg/kg/min and dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min. BP 84/52, lactate 6.2 mmol/L (rising from 4.8 mmol/L), urine output 5 mL/h, peripheries cool and mottled to mid-thigh. He is intubated and ventilated. Echocardiography shows severely impaired left ventricular function (ejection fraction 15%), no mechanical complication, no tamponade. He is 54 years old with no significant comorbidity.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118, BP 84/52, SpO2 92% ventilated, lactate 6.2 mmol/L, urine 5 mL/h.
data_table.Echo: LVEF 15%, no MR, no VSD, no tamponade.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase noradrenaline and add adrenaline infusion
options.rationale: Escalating catecholamine doses worsens myocardial oxygen demand, arrhythmia and lactic acidosis without addressing pump failure. OptimaCC and registry data argue against catecholamine stacking in refractory shock (OptimaCC).

Refractory cardiogenic shock in a young patient without prohibitive comorbidity, with rising lactate and end-organ hypoperfusion despite optimal pharmacological support, is the indication for veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation as a bridge to recovery or definitive therapy. Australian extracorporeal life support centres (eg Royal Prince Alfred, St Vincent's Sydney) accept referrals 24/7.

IABP-SHOCK II showed no mortality benefit for intra-aortic balloon pump in cardiogenic shock after MI. It remains an option only as a temporary bridge when ECMO is unavailable, with limited haemodynamic support (~0.5 L/min) (IABP-SHOCK II).

Milrinone is a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor inodilator; in hypotensive cardiogenic shock it worsens systemic vascular resistance and is not the appropriate escalation. It has a role in pulmonary hypertension complicating right heart failure (NHFA).
PCI was completed 8 hours earlier; repeat angiography is indicated only if a new vessel concern arises. The problem is pump failure, not unaddressed ischaemia (NHFA).

explanation.summary: Refractory cardiogenic shock with rising lactate despite optimal vasoactive support in a young patient without prohibitive comorbidity is an indication for veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation as a bridge to recovery, transplant or durable mechanical support.
key_points: IABP-SHOCK II: intra-aortic balloon pump does not improve mortality
VA-ECMO provides full cardiopulmonary support (4-6 L/min)
Refer early: outcomes degrade once multi-organ failure established
Australian centres operate 24/7 transfer pathways
explanation.context: VA-ECMO drains venous blood, oxygenates it externally and returns it to the arterial system, fully bypassing the heart and lungs. It is a bridge, not destination therapy. Patient selection (age, comorbidity, neurological status, prospects for recovery or transplant) is critical.

stem: A 73-year-old man with known severe aortic stenosis presents to a Central Coast LHD emergency department with acute dyspnoea. He has crackles to the apices bilaterally and is using accessory muscles. BP 86/58, HR 104 sinus, SpO2 88% on 10 L oxygen, RR 30. He is alert. Bedside echocardiography confirms severe aortic stenosis with peak velocity 4.6 m/s and moderately impaired left ventricular function.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104, BP 86/58, SpO2 88% on 10 L oxygen, RR 30.
data_table.Echo: Severe AS (peak V 4.6 m/s), moderately impaired LV.
data_table.Examination: Crackles to apices, accessory muscle use, ejection systolic murmur.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial intervention?

options.rationale: Nitrates in severe aortic stenosis can precipitate profound hypotension because cardiac output is preload-dependent across a fixed obstruction. Vasodilatation is hazardous (eTG).

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg and non-invasive ventilation
options.rationale: In hypotensive pulmonary oedema with severe aortic stenosis, the priority is gentle pulmonary decongestion (loop diuretic) and ventilatory support to reduce work of breathing without dropping preload. Avoid vasodilators and beta-blockers acutely; consider urgent cardiology and cardiothoracic input for valve intervention.

options.text: Intravenous morphine 5 mg and fluid bolus 500 mL
options.rationale: Fluid loading worsens pulmonary oedema; morphine increases mortality in acute heart failure. Both interventions are contraindicated (NHFA).
Beta-blockade in acute decompensated heart failure with severe aortic stenosis and hypotension can precipitate haemodynamic collapse. It is held during acute decompensation (NHFA).

Vasopressors are reserved for shock with low systemic vascular resistance or as a bridge in severe aortic stenosis with critical hypoperfusion. The first step is decongestion plus non-invasive ventilation; vasopressors are an escalation if needed (eTG).

explanation.summary: Acute pulmonary oedema with hypotension and severe aortic stenosis demands cautious preload management: diuresis is required for congestion but vasodilatation is hazardous across the fixed obstruction. Non-invasive ventilation reduces work of breathing. Urgent valve intervention is the definitive treatment.
key_points: Severe AS = preload-dependent; avoid nitrates and aggressive vasodilation
Diuresis is required despite hypotension - congestion will worsen it further
NIV reduces afterload and work of breathing without dropping preload acutely
Definitive treatment is SAVR or TAVI
explanation.context: Severe aortic stenosis is the most common indication for surgical or transcatheter valve replacement in Australia. Decompensated heart failure in this group has high in-hospital mortality without intervention. Acute pharmacological vasodilation, particularly nitrates, is a recognised cause of catastrophic hypotension.

stem: An 82-year-old woman is admitted with community-acquired pneumonia. She has had no chest pain. Heart rate has been 118 sinus for 24 hours and oxygen saturation has dipped to 89% on room air. Routine high-sensitivity troponin returns at 145 ng/L, rising to 210 ng/L on a 3-hour repeat (reference <14). ECG shows sinus tachycardia, no ST changes. She remains afebrile after 24 hours of antibiotics, but is dyspnoeic. Echocardiography shows a structurally normal left ventricle.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118 sinus, BP 124/76, SpO2 89% room air, RR 24.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Treat the underlying pneumonia and hypoxia; do not commence dual antiplatelet therapy
options.rationale: This is type 2 myocardial infarction (supply-demand mismatch from sepsis, hypoxia and tachycardia), not type 1 atherothrombotic infarction. Management targets the precipitant. Antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy in type 2 MI has no proven benefit and carries bleeding harm (Fourth Universal Definition of MI; Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction 2018
Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Commence dual antiplatelet therapy and refer for angiography within 24 hours
options.rationale: The invasive NSTE-ACS pathway applies to type 1 myocardial infarction. Applying it to type 2 risks bleeding without benefit (Fourth Universal Definition).
source_refs: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction 2018

options.rationale: Aspirin in type 2 myocardial infarction has weak evidence and may add bleeding risk in unwell elderly inpatients. Treating the precipitant is the priority (Fourth Universal Definition).
source_refs: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction 2018

options.rationale: Thrombolysis is reserved for ST-elevation infarction when primary PCI is not timely available. It has no role in type 2 myocardial infarction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.rationale: Routine angiography after type 2 myocardial infarction is not indicated unless ongoing symptoms or features suggest underlying coronary disease that would change management. Risk-stratify outpatient with non-invasive imaging if clinically warranted (Fourth Universal Definition).
source_refs: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction 2018

explanation.summary: Type 2 myocardial infarction arises from oxygen supply-demand mismatch (sepsis, anaemia, hypoxia, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia) without atherothrombosis. Management targets the precipitant, not antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy.
key_points: Type 1: plaque rupture, thrombosis; treat with ACS pathway
Type 2: supply-demand mismatch; treat the precipitant
Troponin rise alone does not equal type 1 MI
Routine angiography in type 2 lacks evidence of benefit
explanation.context: The Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction distinguishes five types. Type 2 is increasingly recognised in unwell inpatients and is associated with poor short-term mortality driven by the underlying condition rather than the cardiac event itself.

sources.label: Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction 2018
Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: Post-discharge bridge therapy and clinic follow-up
stem: A 70-year-old woman is discharged after a 5-day admission with decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (ejection fraction 32%). She has been initiated on bisoprolol, perindopril, spironolactone, dapagliflozin and frusemide. She lives alone in a Hunter New England LHD rural town 90 minutes from the cardiology clinic.

data_table.Context: Lives alone, rural, 90 min from cardiology clinic.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up plan?

options.text: Heart failure nurse review within 7 to 14 days and cardiology clinic within 6 weeks
options.rationale: NHFA recommends early post-discharge review within 7 to 14 days by a heart failure nurse or general practitioner for clinical assessment, medication uptitration and reinforcement of self-care, with cardiology review within 6 weeks. Early follow-up reduces 30-day readmission.
Three-month review misses the window when uptitration and decompensation events occur most frequently. Heart failure readmission peaks in the first 30 days (NHFA).

options.text: General practitioner review when symptoms recur
options.rationale: Symptom-triggered follow-up defers titration and self-management education, both of which are key drivers of readmission reduction (NHFA).
Unstructured discharge is associated with 30-day readmission rates over 20%. Multidisciplinary follow-up is standard (NHFA).

options.text: Inpatient referral for cardiac transplantation assessment
options.rationale: Transplantation assessment is reserved for advanced HFrEF (NYHA III-IV despite optimal therapy, recurrent admissions, dependence on inotropic support) in selected younger patients. Her course does not yet warrant referral (NHFA).

explanation.summary: Heart failure 30-day readmission is reduced by multidisciplinary discharge bundles: early nurse review (7 to 14 days), structured education, medication uptitration to target doses, and clear self-care/red-flag advice.
key_points: Early review (7-14 days) by HF nurse or GP
Cardiology review within 6 weeks
Telephone/telehealth substitution acceptable for rural patients
Discharge bundle includes weight diary and reasons-to-call card
explanation.context: Hospital readmission within 30 days affects 20-25% of Australian heart failure discharges and is the single largest cost in chronic heart failure care. Multidisciplinary post-discharge programs (such as the Heart Foundation's Connecting Hearts) demonstrably reduce this.

stem: A 68-year-old man with no prior cardiac history presents with light-headedness and one syncopal episode. ECG shows complete heart block with broad QRS escape at 32 bpm. BP 78/48, HR 32, SpO2 96% room air. He is alert but pale and clammy. Intravenous atropine 600 mcg has been given with no response.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 32, BP 78/48, SpO2 96% room air, RR 18.
data_table.ECG: Complete heart block, broad QRS escape 32 bpm.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.rationale: Atropine has limited effect in infranodal block (broad QRS escape, complete heart block with high-grade nodal disease). Maximum dose is 3 mg total; further atropine is unlikely to work and delays definitive therapy (ANZCOR 11.9).

Unstable bradycardia (hypotension, syncope) unresponsive to atropine is managed with transcutaneous pacing as a bridge to transvenous pacing and permanent pacemaker. Sedation is essential as transcutaneous pacing is painful. Isoprenaline or adrenaline infusion is an alternative chemical bridge.

options.text: Permanent pacemaker insertion in the next routine catheter laboratory list
options.rationale: Routine listing fails to address the immediate unstable bradycardia. Pacing is required now; permanent device follows after stabilisation (ANZCOR 11.9).
Amiodarone slows AV conduction and could worsen the block. It is contraindicated (eTG).
Fluid does not address an electrical conduction block. It may briefly support filling pressure but is not the corrective intervention (eTG).

explanation.summary: Unstable bradycardia from complete heart block requires immediate pacing. Transcutaneous pacing under sedation is the bridge to transvenous pacing and definitive permanent pacemaker. Atropine has limited effect on infranodal block.
key_points: Adverse features (shock, syncope, ischaemia, heart failure) define instability
Atropine max 3 mg total; ineffective in infranodal block
Transcutaneous pacing - sedate, check capture (mechanical and electrical)
Isoprenaline or adrenaline infusion is alternative chemical bridge
explanation.context: Complete heart block with a broad QRS escape suggests block below the AV node (infranodal), often Mobitz II progressing. Such blocks rarely respond to atropine because atropine's effect is on the AV node itself. Pacing - electrical or chemical - is the answer.

subtopic_detail: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection - young female presentation
stem: A 42-year-old woman 3 weeks post-partum presents with 2 hours of chest pain. She has no cardiovascular risk factors. ECG shows 2 mm ST elevation in V3 to V5. She is haemodynamically stable. Urgent coronary angiography reveals a long smooth tapering stenosis in the mid left anterior descending artery without atherosclerotic plaque; intracoronary imaging confirms a Type 2 spontaneous coronary artery dissection. Flow is preserved (TIMI 3). She has no ongoing ischaemia.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Conservative management with aspirin and beta-blocker; admit for observation
options.rationale: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection with preserved flow and no ongoing ischaemia is managed conservatively; most heal spontaneously over 4 to 6 weeks. Percutaneous coronary intervention in spontaneous coronary artery dissection often propagates the dissection. Single antiplatelet (aspirin) and beta-blocker are standard; dual antiplatelet duration is debated and shorter in spontaneous coronary artery dissection than in atherosclerotic ACS.
source_refs: European Society of Cardiology Position Paper on SCAD 2018

options.text: Emergency percutaneous coronary intervention with stent placement
options.rationale: PCI in spontaneous coronary artery dissection has high complication rates (dissection propagation, intramural haematoma extension, stent malapposition once healing occurs). Reserved for ongoing ischaemia, haemodynamic instability or left main involvement (AHA).

Coronary artery bypass grafting in spontaneous coronary artery dissection is technically challenging because of the dissected friable wall and limited landing zones. Reserved for left main spontaneous coronary artery dissection or failed conservative management (AHA).
Thrombolysis in spontaneous coronary artery dissection can propagate the intramural haematoma and is contraindicated (AHA).

options.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy for 12 months and high-intensity statin
options.rationale: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection is not atherosclerotic; statins are not indicated unless other indications exist. Dual antiplatelet duration is shorter than in atherothrombotic ACS, often single antiplatelet alone is preferred (European Society of Cardiology).
source_refs: European Society of Cardiology Position Paper on SCAD 2018

explanation.summary: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection is a non-atherosclerotic cause of acute coronary syndrome, predominantly affecting younger women, peripartum patients and those with fibromuscular dysplasia. Conservative management is preferred unless ongoing ischaemia or instability mandates intervention. Routine percutaneous coronary intervention, thrombolysis and dual antiplatelet duration differ from atherothrombotic care.
key_points: Conservative care: most lesions heal in 4-6 weeks
PCI reserved for ongoing ischaemia/instability; high complication rate
Thrombolysis contraindicated (haematoma propagation)
Screen for fibromuscular dysplasia in extracoronary beds
explanation.context: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection accounts for up to 35% of acute coronary syndrome in women under 50 in Australian registries. The mechanism is intramural haematoma in the coronary wall rather than plaque rupture, which is why the standard atherosclerotic ACS toolkit (stenting, lytic therapy, long dual antiplatelet) does not apply.

sources.label: European Society of Cardiology Position Paper on SCAD 2018
